narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 36, which is close to the dataset average of 36.70, you've put together a respectable academic profile for law school admission. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your probability of failing the bar at 44%, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor outcomes. This prediction wasn't driven by your LSAT or GPA alone but by a combination of factors from your early academic record that raised concerns about your long-term performance. The three most influential elements in this decision were your LSAT score, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the average of 5.53), and your Year 1 decile of 1 (compared to the typical 5.71 average). Among these, your LSAT stood out as the strongest positive signal, pushing the prediction toward bar exam success because it's competitively close to what successful students typically score. In contrast, your lower Year 3 decile ranking pulled notably toward failure, suggesting the model saw it as a sign of potential struggles in later undergraduate years relative to peers. Similarly, your bottom-decile Year 1 performance contributed toward the failure prediction, indicating early challenges that often correlate with bar exam difficulties in the data. Compared to typical admitted students, who tend to rank much higher in school deciles throughout their undergrad years, your early and mid-year standings were key drags that outweighed your other strengths. While your full-time status and middle-income background aligned with averages and didn't sway things much, they couldn't offset those academic flags. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it higher or strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile perceptions. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing early weaknesses head-on, so reviewing your Year 1 and 3 transcripts for patterns could guide targeted improvements before reapplying. With persistence, you can shift the model's view in your favor next time.
